course_description:
Globally, telehealth is a popular, clinically effective communication vehicle that is rapidly becoming mainstream. This course introduces you to the key concepts relating to the use of telehealth to improve patient-centred clinical care. Course material covers the historical development of clinical telehealth in Australia and the contributions of other significant research innovators. A range of clinical telehealth applications will be explored using case examples that are relevant to today's health priority areas. You will review relevant guidelines and standards relating to telehealth integration and everyday practice of telehealth to provide equitable access to healthcare irrespective of the consumer's geographical location.

HLTH2001 is an introductory course in the professional practice of clinical telehealth and it's current implementation into service delivery models (both in Australia and internationally.)ﾠThe course will provide you with an understanding of what clinical telehealth is and the ' when, how and why ' it is currently used to address equitable-access-to healthcare issues. Diverse case studies are examined to highlight the effectiveness of clinical telehealth across medical, allied health, and multidisciplinary team service models. Examples include emergency and acute medicine, chronic disease management, outpatient services, hospital care, aged care, transition care, primary care, rehabilitation, disability services, and wellness models. The course looks at what makes a telehealth service successful and why one might fail - some important considerations when setting up a clinical telehealth service are explored. Technology considerations, human resources, economics and government policies relating to telehealth will all be examined as part of this. This course will set you on the path to thinking critically about the benefits and challenges of telehealth- fromﾠindividual, clinical and organisational perspectives. ﾠ

course_contacts.name: Medical School Student and Academic Administration

course_aims: This course introduces students to the use of clinical telehealth as a means of delivering more equitable healthcare in areas where access to health services may be lacking. Students will examine the types of equipment that can be used to perform telehealth, and learn about standards and guidelines to consider when conducting a telehealth consultation. The course covers aspects such as the history of telehealth, definitions and current applications and explores the privacy, security and medico-legal aspects that need to be considered.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand basic concepts, theories, terminologies and history relating to clinical telehealth
Describe types of telehealth and their role in the clinical setting
Understand the role of clinical telehealth in the Australian and global context
Outline the factors for consideration when setting up a facility for clinical telehealth
Describe the factors to consider when conducting clinical telehealth consultations
Discuss the privacy, security and medico-legal issues with clinical telehealth

assessment_summary.title: Assessment Task 1: Perceptions of Telehealth (Parts A and B)
assessment_summary.due_date: Assessment Task 1: Part A 21/03/2025 5:00 pm; Assessment Task 1: Part B 4/04/2025 5:00 pm; Assessment Task 1 (parts A and B) runs during Weeks 3-6

assessment_details.title: Assessment Task 1: Perceptions of Telehealth (Parts A and B)
assessment_details.due_date: Assessment Task 1: Part A 21/03/2025 5:00 pm Assessment Task 1: Part B 4/04/2025 5:00 pm Assessment Task 1 (parts A and B) runs during Weeks 3-6
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Task 1: Perceptions of Telehealth Detailed instructions and marking criteria will be available to you on the Learn.UQ (Blackboard) Course Assessment page during Teaching Week 2. All tasks associated with this Assessment must be completed by the end of Teaching Week 5. We use the Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for this online activity. During Week 2 you will be randomly assigned to a small group (2 or 3 members). Each group will then be emailed a set of questions and prompts to conduct an interview. Task 1: Individual interviews on telehealth experience (Individual task - worth 10/30 marks) • To be conducted prior to Teaching Week 4/Task 2 • You will conduct and take notes of a quick interview with 5 friends or family members (can be from Australia or overseas) who have used telehealth in the past 5 years about their opinion and experience using any modality of telehealth (include mobile health, telehealth consultations using phone or video, teledermatology, teleradiology, etc). • Questions and prompts will be emailed to you, and the group can choose extra questions they might find useful, if they agree on those before conducting the interviews. • You will upload your raw data collected from 5 interviews via Learn.UQ (Blackboard) using the template provided. Task 2: Group analysis, discussion, reflection and summary of findings (Group task - worth 20/30 marks) • To be conducted during Teaching Weeks 4 and/or 5 • Groups will arrange to meet in person or online to analyse their tabulated responses and discuss the benefits/challenges of using telehealth for clinical practice. • Specific topic areas to be discussed/template for summarising findings will be provided with the instructions. • Summary of findings to be uploaded via Learn.UQ (Blackboard) Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Via Learn.UQ (Blackboard)
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Assessments submitted after the due date without an approved extension will receive a late penalty.

assessment_details.task_description: This multiple-choice quiz is designed to test your knowledge and understanding of learning associated with Week 1 to Week 7 material. The assessment will contribute up to 30% of your total marks for the course. The multiple-choice quiz comprise 30 questions, each question is worth 1 mark. This is an online timed quiz that will be made available via the Learn.UQ (Blackboard) Course Assessment page. It is compulsory that you attempt this quiz. You must complete it in one sitting, you cannot save and return to it at a later date. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 3
learning_activities.topic: Module 1: Clinical telehealth - an overview Covers Weeks 1 - 3 of teaching. It offers a comprehensive understanding of telehealth, encompassing the definition of telehealth, tracing its historical origins and evolution into modern healthcare, and examining both initial and recent benefits. Module 1 introduces you to how telehealth is linked to ensuring fair access to healthcare for patients, aligning with the Australian Charter of Healthcare Rights, addressing the Quadruple Aim of Healthcare, and contribution to the achievement of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 3 - Good Health & Well-being. Week 1: What is Telehealth? - What is telehealth and related terms (telemedicine, telecare, e-health and online health), what is clinical telehealth and driver for telehealth Week 2: Telehealth - the past and the future - History of telehealth and funding related changes since COVID in Australia Week 3: Benefits of telehealth - Clinical, service and economic benefits of Telehealth, stressing the opportunity to improve access to care Refer to the Course 'Learning Resources' on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for links to all lectures, Weekly Study Guide, required and recommended reading. Content is organised as Module and then by Teaching Week. Content is typically uploaded to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) on the Friday prior to each Teaching Week. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 8
learning_activities.topic: Module 2: Telehealth in action Teaching Weeks 4-8 explores the different telehealth approaches with respect to delivering healthcare i.e. store and forward models (i.e. asynchronous communication via email, secure messaging, encrypted software,) real-time models (i.e. synchronous communication - video or phone based), mobile-device based models (including remote monitoring/wearables) and hybrid models of care (i.e. a blend of telehealth and face-to-face) Week 4: Approaches to telehealth - an overview - Overview of telehealth briefly explaining all models possible, various classifications and terminologies per specialty Week 5: Telehealth in practice - real-time models - Overview of real-time models, and video and phone consultations Week 6: Telehealth in practice - store and forward models - Overview of store and forward models, teledermatology and eConsults Week 7: Telehealth in practice - mobile health models - Overview of mobile health and potential application for remote patient monitoring Week 8: Telehealth in practice - hybrid models of care - What are models of care, hybrid models, care pathways, the importance of attending to patient needs. Refer to the Course 'Learning Resources' on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for links to all lectures, Weekly Study Guide, required and recommended reading. Content is organised as Module and then by Teaching Week. Content is typically uploaded to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) on the Friday prior to each Teaching Week. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 13
learning_activities.topic: Module 3: Planning & implenting telehealth Teaching Weeks 9-12 outlines practical considerations, technical requirements, planning for a telehealth program and medico-legal considerations. Week 13 is reserved for review of key concepts covered over the Semester that will assist students with final assessment. Week 9: Planning for telehealth - Telehealth Etiquette and Technology for video conferencing Week 10: Privacy considerations and sustainability of telehealth - Privacy & Security in Telehealth, Sustaining telehealth beyond COVID-19 Week 11: Telehealth - future potential - Potential of COVIU, telestroke and Tele-ICU Week 12: Telehealth - a global perspective - Telehealth around the world, including case studies from Denmark, Brazil and Japan Week 13: Review Refer to the Course 'Learning Resources' on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for links to all lectures, Weekly Study Guide, required and recommended reading. Weeks 9-12 content will be available from Teaching Week 9 to assist students with final assessment preparation. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
